attributes.stem:
An anaesthetics review is requested for a 52-year-old in the post-operative care unit who is experiencing new neurological symptoms. She is in recovery following a laparoscopic cholecystectomy that was converted to open due to difficult anatomy and bleeding. The operation took five hours, during which she required two units of packed red blood cells for an estimated blood loss of 800 mL. She became hypotensive during the procedure, with her systolic blood pressure dropping to 85 mmHg for approximately 20 minutes before responding to fluid resuscitation and vasopressors. She has a history of hypertension and migraine, for which she takes amlodipine and sumatriptan, but is otherwise well and independent from home with family.

The review is requested because she is now complaining of weakness and altered sensation in her right hand. On examination, she appears tired but well. Her pulse rate is 95/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. There is reduced sensation in her right little finger and the medial half of her ring finger. She has difficulty spreading her fingers apart and appears to have weakness of thumb adduction. The rest of her neurological examination is normal. Her surgical wound appears clean and her abdomen is soft.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Despite the complicated intraoperative course, this patient's neurological symptoms are most consistent with ulnar nerve compression injury. The characteristic distribution of sensory changes (medial 1.5 digits) and weakness of intrinsic hand muscles (particularly finger abduction and thumb adduction) are hallmark features of this.

The prolonged surgical time, conversion to open procedure, and potential positioning changes during the complicated surgery all increased the risk of positioning-related nerve injury. The ulnar nerve is particularly vulnerable to compression at the elbow when the arm is flexed and externally rotated.

Acute cerebellar stroke would typically present with ataxia, vertigo, nystagmus, or dysmetria, rather than isolated sensory and motor deficits in a peripheral nerve distribution. While the period of intraoperative hypotension could theoretically cause watershed infarction, the pattern of deficits does not fit with a central nervous system lesion.

Migraine with aura can cause neurological symptoms, but these typically include visual disturbances, sensory symptoms that spread gradually, and resolve within 60 minutes. The focal nature of the deficits in a specific nerve distribution makes migraine an unlikely cause.

Brachial plexus injury would typically cause more extensive neurological deficits involving multiple nerve distributions in the upper limb, rather than the specific pattern seen here affecting only the ulnar nerve distribution.

Brachial arterial embolism could be considered given the intraoperative blood loss and hypotension, but would present with a cold, painful limb with absent pulses, rather than isolated neurological deficits in a specific nerve distribution.
learningPoints: Ulnar nerve compression presents with sensory changes in the medial 1.5 digits and weakness of intrinsic hand muscles.
Post-operative neurological symptoms require prompt assessment to differentiate positioning injury from other causes.
Intraoperative complications can mask or complicate the assessment of post-operative neurological symptoms.
references: Schubert, A. Positioning Injuries in Anesthesia: An Update. Advances in Anesthesia, Volume 26, 31 - 65. Available from: https://www.advancesinanesthesia.com/article/S0737-6146(08)00010-5
Winfree CJ, Kline DG. Intraoperative positioning nerve injuries. Surg Neurol. 2005 Jan;63(1):5-18; discussion 18. doi: 10.1016/j.surneu.2004.03.024. PMID: 15639509.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
